IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—building deployable natural language-to-SQL systems with minimal annotation—by showing neural seq2seq models can map directly to full SQL and be improved via a user-feedback + selective crowd-annotation loop. Empirical results (comparable accuracy on GEO880/ATIS, and a live deployment reaching 63.51% correct after three cycles) demonstrate the approach is viable and could change how practitioners bootstrap NLIDBs. The interactive learning and schema-driven augmentation components open substantive follow-on work on weakly supervised, user-in-the-loop semantic parsing, and have applicability beyond this subfield. Noise and execution failures are notable limitations but do not negate the method’s practical significance. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of quickly bootstrapping NL-to-SQL systems for new domains by showing neural seq2seq models can map utterances directly to full SQL and be improved via a practical feedback-and-crowd-annotation loop. Empirically it achieves comparable accuracy to prior work on GEO880 and ATIS and demonstrates a live deployment that learned from scratch to answer 63.51% of user questions after three cycles, indicating practical impact for practitioners. The interactive learning, schema-driven templates and anonymization approach open useful applied research directions for low-effort domain adaptation, though noisy user feedback and execution failures limit immediate broad applicability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—neural semantic parsing and natural language interfaces to databases—using methods (seq2seq with attention, data augmentation, anonymization) and evaluations (execution-based accuracy on GEO880 and ATIS, comparisons to prior work) that are standard for top venues. The interactive, feedback-driven deployment and focus on reducing annotation effort make it highly relevant to both academic and applied NLP/ML audiences. Overall the problem formulation, datasets, metrics, and positioning are well aligned with the expectations of a top-tier NLP/ML conference. | B(5): This paper squarely addresses a core NLP/ML topic—semantic parsing and natural language interfaces to databases—using neural sequence models and an interactive feedback loop, which is of clear interest to top-tier NLP/ML conferences. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS), uses execution-based accuracy and user-judged correctness, and compares to relevant prior work, matching expected methods and evaluation standards. The deployment-focused contributions (user feedback, selective crowd annotation, schema-driven data augmentation) are well positioned for the venue’s audience concerned with practical and empirical advances in semantic parsing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong: the model is evaluated on standard benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) with execution-based accuracy comparisons to prior work and a real-world three-stage online deployment showing progressive improvement. Some sensitivity analysis is present (effects of schema-driven templates and PPDB paraphrasing, and quantified user-feedback noise rates), and limitations such as noisy feedback and template shortcomings are explicitly discussed. Related work coverage is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and explicit, systematic ablation studies beyond the reported template/paraphrase observations are not clearly described, leaving minor gaps. | B(4): The experiments are solid: evaluation on standard benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) with execution-based accuracy, comparisons to prior work, and a real-world three-stage online deployment (63.51% user-correct after three cycles) support the main claims. The paper reports ablations tied to data augmentation (templates/PPDB/entity anonymization) and documents user-feedback noise rates (6.1%/6.3% mislabels, 22.3% non-executable outputs) and template limitations on ATIS, showing attention to failure modes. Related work is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, but there are minor gaps—sensitivity analyses around noisy feedback handling and deeper ablations (and no mention of code release) which would strengthen reproducibility and robustness claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper combines existing neural seq2seq parsing (biLSTM + attention) and data augmentation/paraphrasing techniques with a practical, feedback-driven deployment loop to learn NL→SQL directly, which yields comparable accuracy to prior work on GEO880 and ATIS. While mapping directly to full SQL and using binary user feedback plus selective crowd annotation for continual improvement is a useful applied contribution, these components largely assemble known methods rather than introduce a fundamentally new model or theoretical perspective. Thus the contribution is a moderate, application-oriented novelty (incremental) that would be informative but not surprising to experts. | B(4): The paper combines several known components (seq2seq neural generation, schema-driven synthetic data, paraphrasing) but brings a notable new perspective by directly mapping natural language to full SQL and coupling this with an interactive, feedback-driven deployment loop that uses binary user feedback plus selective crowd annotation to rapidly bootstrap and improve parsers in new domains. The approach is clearly differentiated from prior work (benchmarked against GEO880/ATIS and contrasted with earlier systems) and the live three-stage deployment demonstrating 63.51% correctness provides empirical novelty beyond typical offline evaluations. Overall this is a non-trivial, practically useful extension of existing techniques rather than a purely incremental reuse, but it does not introduce a wholly new theoretical paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines existing neural seq2seq parsing (biLSTM + attention) and data augmentation/paraphrasing techniques with a practical, feedback-driven deployment loop to learn NL→SQL directly, which yields comparable accuracy to prior work on GEO880 and ATIS. While mapping directly to full SQL and using binary user feedback plus selective crowd annotation for continual improvement is a useful applied contribution, these components largely assemble known methods rather than introduce a fundamentally new model or theoretical perspective. Thus the contribution is a moderate, application-oriented novelty (incremental) that would be informative but not surprising to experts.

The approach is clearly differentiated from prior work (benchmarked against GEO880/ATIS and contrasted with earlier systems) and the live three-stage deployment demonstrating 63.51% correctness provides empirical novelty beyond typical offline evaluations. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: comparable accuracy on GEO880 and ATIS to prior work, a live deployment reaching 63.51% after three cycles, and improvements from schema-driven templates/paraphrasing and anonymization. There are no mathematical proofs to verify and the modeling approach (seq2seq with attention) is standard and correctly applied. Experimental methodology is largely sound—appropriate execution-based accuracy, reasonable baselines, and real-world deployment—but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and leaves some generalization questions about template/paraphrase augmentation. Overall the conclusions are justified by the presented results, with only minor methodological evidence gaps. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are supported by empirical evidence: a seq2seq model trained with schema-driven templates and paraphrasing achieves comparable execution-based accuracy on GEO880 and ATIS and a live three-stage deployment reached 63.51% user-correct answers, with detailed reporting of hyperparameters and baselines. Experimental methodology appears largely sound (proper dataset baselines and execution-based evaluation), but the summary indicates some practical weaknesses—no mention of statistical significance testing, substantial noisy feedback (≈6% false labels and 22.3% non-executing queries), and template limitations on ATIS—that could affect robustness. No formal mathematical derivations were required or presented. Overall the technical claims are credible but there are minor methodological concerns that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: comparable accuracy on GEO880 and ATIS to prior work, a live deployment reaching 63.51% after three cycles, and improvements from schema-driven templates/paraphrasing and anonymization. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, including the interactive feedback loop and direct SQL generation claim. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and well-defined given the detailed methodology (encoder–decoder model, anonymization, templates, iterative deployment) and reported hyperparameters. Figures/tables/examples seem informative since experiments report execution-based accuracy, user-judged correctness, and annotation-effort statistics, and limitations (e.g., noisy user feedback, template shortcomings on ATIS) are explicitly discussed. Overall the writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from motivation through model, deployment loop, and experiments (datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are all explicitly reported). The main contributions are stated early and the methodological details (encoder–decoder architecture, anonymization, template/paraphrase augmentation, and iterative feedback/annotation loop) are described clearly, suggesting consistent notation. Results are easy to follow because execution-based accuracy, user-judged correctness, and annotation-effort metrics are used and limitations (noisy feedback, template shortcomings) are openly discussed. Overall the writing quality is good, though a few minor presentation details could be tightened for perfection.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (e.g., Dong and Lapata 2016; Jia and Liang 2016; earlier parsers such as Zettlemoyer & Collins 2007 and Poon 2013). Comparisons are made on standard datasets (GEO880, ATIS) using execution-based accuracy and user-judged correctness, matching established evaluation conditions. The authors explicitly note when prior methods perform comparably and discuss limitations (e.g., templates not helping ATIS, noisy user feedback), and the summary states that related work coverage is thorough and differences (direct SQL output, feedback loop, data augmentation) are analyzed. | B(5): The paper compares to relevant and recent baselines (e.g., Dong & Lapata 2016; Jia & Liang 2016; GUSP/GUSP++; Zettlemoyer & Collins 2007) and evaluates on the same benchmarks (GEO880, ATIS) using execution-based accuracy. It acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably, discusses limitations (e.g., template failures on ATIS) and analyzes how its direct-to-SQL approach differs from prior work. Related work coverage is described as thorough, supporting that comparisons are comprehensive and fair.

main_claims: Neural sequence models can be adapted to map natural language utterances directly to full SQL, bypassing intermediate meaning representations.

A feedback-based interactive learning loop that combines user binary feedback and selective crowd annotation enables rapid deployment and continual improvement of semantic parsers for new domains with minimal intervention.

The proposed model achieves comparable accuracy to prior work on benchmark datasets (GEO880 and ATIS) despite directly producing SQL, and a live three-stage online deployment learned a parser from scratch that answered 63.51% of users' questions correctly after three cycles.
Data augmentation via schema-driven templates and paraphrasing (PPDB) plus entity anonymization improve initial performance and reduce annotation effort as the system is iteratively trained.
_summary.methodology: Train an encoder-decoder neural sequence-to-sequence model (bidirectional LSTM encoder, global attention decoder) to predict anonymized SQL from anonymized utterances using synthetic schema-template data and paraphrases, then iteratively deploy the model, collect binary user feedback on executed query results, add correct model outputs to training data, and send incorrect cases for crowd annotation to retrain the model.
metrics: execution-based accuracy (execute predicted SQL and compare results)
percentage of examples requiring annotation (annotation effort)
limitations: User feedback is noisy: 6.1% of correct generated queries were marked wrong and 6.3% of incorrect queries were marked correct, and 22.3% of generated queries did not execute.
Templates did not improve accuracy on ATIS because many ATIS queries involve two entities while templates generated single-entity questions.
Erroneous user feedback can lead to redundant or incorrect annotations that may deteriorate model accuracy over time.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic parsing / natural language interfaces to databases
